extra.reasoning: rec_drug_a is written only by r2 (fires on seizure_history AND elderly); rec_drug_b is written only by r3 (fires on seizure_history). These two rules target different outputs, so their equal priority 50 never creates a conflict — priority/tie-breaking only matters among rules writing the SAME output. With seizure_history=true and elderly=true, r2 fires setting rec_drug_a:=true and r3 fires setting rec_drug_b:=true, so both are true simultaneously. asthma is irrelevant (r1 only affects rec_drug_c); set false. This makes (rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b)=true, violating the mutual-exclusion property. Trace for seizure_history=true, elderly=true, asthma=false: r1 inactive -> rec_drug_c=false (default); r2 active -> rec_drug_a=true; r3 active -> rec_drug_b=true.
